LOW.explanation: may indicate iron deficiency, vitamin deficiency, or mild anemia
recommendations: Discuss with your doctor to evaluate iron and vitamin B12 levels.
Consider dietary adjustments or iron supplements under medical advice.

HIGH.explanation: can be associated with dehydration, smoking, or chronic lung conditions
recommendations: Ensure adequate hydration.
Consult a healthcare provider for further investigation.

LOW.explanation: may indicate anemia, fluid overload, or active blood loss
recommendations: Discuss these results with a healthcare provider for a complete blood evaluation.
Identify any potential nutritional gaps in iron or vitamins.

HIGH.explanation: can be associated with dehydration, low blood oxygen, or living at high altitudes
recommendations: Avoid dehydration by drinking sufficient water.
Consult a physician for further evaluation of blood oxygenation.

LOW.explanation: can be associated with viral infections, bone marrow issues, or certain medications
recommendations: Protect yourself from infections and wash hands frequently.
Consult a doctor to review any medications or identify underlying causes.

HIGH.explanation: may indicate active infection, inflammation, stress, or tissue injury
recommendations: Monitor for other signs of infection such as fever or pain.
Seek medical evaluation to localize and treat the source of infection.

LOW.explanation: may indicate increased destruction or decreased production of platelets, raising bleeding risks
recommendations: Avoid activities that carry a risk of bleeding or bruising.
Consult a physician for a hematology review.

HIGH.explanation: can be associated with inflammation, iron deficiency, or active infection
recommendations: Seek doctor consultation to assess if there is underlying chronic inflammation.

LOW.explanation: may indicate hypoglycemia, possibly due to prolonged fasting or insulin imbalance
recommendations: Consume a fast-acting source of sugar (e.g., fruit juice, candy).
Consult your physician to adjust any glucose-lowering medications.

HIGH.explanation: is commonly seen in prediabetes, diabetes, or transient physiological stress
recommendations: Discuss these results with a healthcare provider for HbA1c testing.
Monitor dietary intake of refined carbohydrates and sugars.

CRITICAL.explanation: indicates severely abnormal blood sugar levels that require immediate attention
recommendations: Seek immediate medical attention or consult an emergency care provider.

LOW.explanation: may indicate low protein intake, overhydration, or liver issues
recommendations: Review dietary protein intake with a nutritionist.
Discuss liver function tests with your healthcare provider.

HIGH.explanation: can be associated with decreased kidney clearance or high protein intake
recommendations: Ensure adequate hydration to support kidney function.
Discuss with a physician to evaluate overall kidney function.

LOW.explanation: may indicate decreased muscle mass or overhydration
recommendations: Maintain adequate physical activity and dietary protein.
Discuss with your physician if you have underlying chronic conditions.

HIGH.explanation: can be associated with reduced kidney filtration or temporary dehydration
recommendations: Drink plenty of fluids and re-evaluate.
Schedule a follow-up kidney function test (renal profile).

HIGH.explanation: may be associated with increased cardiovascular risk, calling for dietary review
recommendations: Consider a low-cholesterol, low-saturated-fat diet.
Discuss with a doctor regarding lipid profile follow-ups and lifestyle changes.

BORDERLINE.explanation: is slightly elevated, suggesting monitoring and lifestyle adjustments
recommendations: Focus on healthy fats (e.g., olive oil, nuts) and regular physical exercise.
Recheck cholesterol levels in 3 to 6 months.

HIGH.explanation: may indicate elevated fat levels, sometimes associated with metabolic syndrome or dietary intake
recommendations: Limit sugar and alcohol intake.
Incorporate regular cardiovascular exercises into your weekly routine.

BORDERLINE.explanation: is borderline high, recommending dietary modifications
recommendations: Reduce refined carbohydrates and increase dietary fiber.
Discuss metabolic health with a healthcare provider.

HIGH.explanation: may indicate increased plaque accumulation risk in arteries
recommendations: Consult a doctor to evaluate cardiovascular risk factors.
Consider lifestyle improvements, including reduction of saturated fats.

BORDERLINE.explanation: is borderline high, indicating potential benefit from diet optimization
recommendations: Incorporate cholesterol-lowering foods (e.g., oats, beans) into your diet.

LOW.explanation: may indicate reduced protection against cardiovascular deposits
recommendations: Increase physical activity, which can help raise HDL levels.
Avoid smoking and consume healthy fats (e.g., omega-3).
Consult an endocrinologist for thyroid evaluation (FT3/FT4 levels).
Consult a healthcare provider for thyroid hormone evaluation and follow-up.

HIGH.explanation: is commonly seen in prediabetes or diabetes, reflecting average blood sugar levels over the past 3 months
recommendations: Schedule a consultation with a physician or endocrinologist.
Monitor daily sugar intake and incorporate low-glycemic foods.

BORDERLINE.explanation: indicates prediabetic levels, suggesting early dietary and lifestyle action
recommendations: Increase physical exercise to improve insulin sensitivity.
Limit sugary beverages and process foods.

LOW.explanation: is lower than the standard reference range
recommendations: Consult a doctor to evaluate potential underlying causes.

HIGH.explanation: is higher than the standard reference range
recommendations: Consult a doctor to evaluate potential underlying causes.

CRITICAL.explanation: is critically outside the standard reference range
recommendations: Seek prompt medical advice from a qualified healthcare provider.

BORDERLINE.explanation: is slightly outside the optimal range
recommendations: Monitor these levels and discuss lifestyle modifications with your doctor.
